@graph.description: Consent forms present: No. Consent covers future access/use: Other: no consent forms exist.

@graph.name: Currently untitled but will sit the library Local History collection
@graph.description:
110 cassette tapes created by the former South Melbourne and Port Melbourne Historical Society. They did not merge with any other society. The majority of recordings are from older members of the community describing their life and key events in the area. About half seem to be recorded presentations at the historical society meetings, not interviews. Some of the cassette tapes have dates and include the name of the speaker of interviewer/s but there is limited metadata available about the interview beyond the name of the speaker. 
In addition, 20 digital mp3 recordings on USBs in a drawer in Heritage Centre staff room. File name includes the name of interviewee and year, sometimes full date. Some of the names are the same as on the cassette tapes.  The digital tapes seem to be mostly interviewed by a local history librarian and constitute a separate project to the majority of the cassette tapes by the South Melbourne and Port Melbourne historical society. Only two of the digital interviews have slates so the interviewer is mostly unknown.
@graph.ldac:dateFreeText: Recording period: 1980s, 1990s, 2000s; Historical eras discussed: Pre-1920s, 1920s, 1930s, 1940s, 1950s, 1960s, 1970s, 1980s, 1990s
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: Record memories of older society and key community members to capture the lived experiences, not generally recorded in published books. There are also people speaking on historical subjects e.g. 'Health Inspection in Victoria 1800-1980" "Prince of Buskers - Frank Ottenson" "Interview Mr Vin McNamara 85 year old 176 Heath Street Port Melbourne 22/10/1989 (1 1/8 sides)" "Melbourne and Suburbs South Melbourne, Richmond, Yarra River, Life on the Goldfields, Garbage disposal and sewage industrial waste", "Aboriginals of SM. Marilyn Truscott", "Chinese in Australia guest speaker: Morag Loh 9 December 1982"
custom:storageLocation: External digital storage (e.g., USB, hard drive)
@graph.custom:audioFormat: Compact Cassette Tapes, Digital Audio Files (WAV, mp3, etc)
@graph.custom:outputs: No current outputs that I am aware of however library staff member whose role includes curating and providing guides in our popular Guided Walks program is interested in hearing the 1985 tape "How Emerald Hill was saved" with guest speaker Reg Macey at a 11 July South Melbourne - Port Melbourne Histocial and Preservation Society meeting as they are doing a walking tour around public housing "Housing the People of South Melbourne" later in the year.
@graph.custom:otherInfo:
These tapes were found in an unmarked drawer in a locked 'Local History Room' in Port Melbourne Library/town Hall complex. The room suffered from dampness three years ago when the roof of the Town Hall roof collapsed. The next 6 months we'll be launching a 'digitisation station' for cassette tapes. This will be available to the public to convert cassettes from analogue to digital. We aim to make reasonable efforts to identify and contact rights holders before converting making oral histories accessible online e.g., through raising with three existing historical societies in Port Phillip, through library and historical society newsletters. the online catalogue will have takedown provisions. Then convert the tapes to digital and make them accessible on a new module Spydus Archives, which will be searchable on the public library catalogue. Work is being done on the development of a long-term digital preservation strategy and as advised by the IT team will require investment beyond the library’s existing capacity.
 
The launch and maintenance of an accessible digital collection requires: Dedicated staff time for rights clearance, metadata creation, and cataloguing, ongoing maintenance of digital files and public access systems as well as a sustainable model for curation and care of growing digital content. Without increased staff support or collaboration across Council departments (e.g. with Records, IT and state and national bodies, the risk of neglect or deterioration of the digital collection remains significant. As convenor of the Local Studies Special Interest Group, I am coordinating a professional visit to the State Library of Victoria’s Digitisation area in September. This visit aims to build sector knowledge around scalable and sustainable practices for community-based digitisation projects. Insights from this tour will inform local advocacy and planning.

@graph.name: Collection #7
@graph.description: The Museum’s oral history collection comprises over 700 recorded interviews on cassette tape, along with printed transcripts and, in some cases, summaries. Key themes explored in the collection include the role of women, migration, and local industries such as munitions work, the Sunshine Harvester Works, and chaff mills. Notable subjects include The Life and Times of George Seelaf and 'The Lifeblood of Footscray,' project which focuses on the meat industry.
@graph.ldac:dateFreeText: Recording period: 1980s, 1990s, 2000s, 2010s, 2020s; Historical eras discussed: Pre-1920s, 1920s, 1930s, 1940s, 1950s, 1960s, 1970s, 1980s, 1990s, 2000s
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: The purpose of the oral history collection at Melbourne’s Living Museum of the West is to document and preserve the lived experiences, memories, and voices of communities in Melbourne’s west. As a community-driven ecomuseum, the Museum is committed to capturing the region’s social, industrial, and environmental histories through stories and knowledge that are often intangible and otherwise at risk of being lost. The oral history collection plays an important role in this mission, serving as a key component of the Museum’s evolving public collection. By gathering first-hand accounts (through interviews, transcripts, and recordings) the Museum aims to support communities and audiences to meaningfully connect with, reflect on, and better understand the past, present, and future of this diverse and dynamic region.
@graph.usageInfo: Copyright status: Varies Across the Collection
@graph.custom:audioFormat: Compact Cassette Tapes, Audio CDs, Digital Audio Files (WAV, mp3, etc)
@graph.custom:outputs: Melbourne’s Living Museum of the West has drawn on its oral history collection across more than four decades of community-engaged practice. The collection has informed a wide range of outputs, including books, exhibitions, public programs, interpretive materials, and digital resources. It has also been accessed by external researchers, artists, and community groups for independent projects. Key examples inlcude "The Lifeblood of Footscray: Working Lives at the Angliss Meatworks (1986)," edited by Chris Healy, a publication developed directly from oral history interviews, and the Pipemakers Park Audio Trail, which features edited excerpts from interviews and is integrated into interpretive signage throughout Pipemakers Park in Maribyrnong. The Museum’s collection has also supported numerous artist-led and community history projects, many of which have generated pamphlets, audio works, and exhibitions. Given the community-driven nature of the Museum and the longstanding use of oral histories in its programming, the collection has been foundational to the Museum’s creative and historical output for more than forty years.

@graph.description: The Local History Archive collects materials that document the history of the City of Casey region, including, butnot limited to, the councils and their predecessors, from the earliest records of knowledge. The archive has one sub-collection: the reference collection of published works, alongside thousands of unpublished works. The materials held in the archive have enduring, permanent significance and are used extensively by Local History Librarians to develop programs and displays, respond to enquiries and advise local organisations and councils on historical and heritage matters.
@graph.ldac:dateFreeText: Recording period: 1980s, 1990s; Historical eras discussed: Unsure
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: The archive itself began as a repository from the former City of Berwick in 1990 and was subsequently handed over to Casey Cardinia Libraries Corporation (predecessor of Connected Libraries) in 1994 following the Council’s restructure. The local history archive acquires, manages and preserves objects relating to the past, present and future of the areas encompassed by the current City of Casey (including neighbouring areas), their communities, notable families and individuals, businesses, associations and organisations.

Accessibility has also been an issue, as recordings have not been adequately reviewed and edited to be suited to a public platform. We have been able to share them by request on an individual basis.

@graph.name: Memories of Yallourn
@graph.description: This project records the stories of power industry workers and their families relating to Victoria’s first brown coal generated power station at Yallourn that was established in 1921 and the State Electricity Commission of Victoria's model company town of Yallourn which was completely razed to the ground by the 1980s to allow forfurther mining. The project also records the memories of the Yallourn Medical and Hospital Society, one of the first contributory health schemes to operate in Australia. In 1926 Dr James Moore Andrew took the position of the Society’s first resident medical officer and with his wife Catherine Dorothy Andrew established a family at Yallourn, practising at Yallourn Hospital and lived in Yallourn until his death in 1972. Twenty individual audio interviews were carried out with Yallourn workers and recorded in their homes along with photographs. These are now stored in mp3 audio files on Old Gippstown computers.
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose:
1. Preservation of the history of the region and especially of the township of Yallourn which was removed in the 1980s to make way for additional coal mining 
2. Reinvigoration of displays will lead to further visits to Old Gippsland and to increased tourist spending in the region 
3. Increased knowledge of the power industry, the township of Yallourn and surrounding towns, the provision of health care for SECV workers and their families, and of labour and migrant history 
4. Increased sense of pride in heritage and identification with that past, as well as increased sense of unity through knowledge of shared background 
5. Retention of resources within the community, with recordings being lodged in the Yallourn house at Old Gippstown and on the Old Gippstown buildings website, as well as at Moe and District Historical Society. This will strengthen the sense of ownership of the past and ensure ease of access by local residents and researchers.
@graph.custom:outputs: Memories of Yallourn brochure
@graph.custom:otherInfo: Collections management at Old Gippstown have concerns that this worthwhile project may not be recognised for its importance in the future and consequently be lost.

@graph.name: Collection protocol for Interviews to celebrate Mordialloc Primary School's 125th Anniversary
@graph.description: Consent forms present: Yes. Consent covers future access/use: Yes.

@graph.name: Interviews to celebrate Mordialloc Primary School's 125th Anniversary
@graph.description:
Interviews of past students, parents and teachers at Mordialloc Primary School recorded on cassettes in 1993, beginning with the then oldest surviving student 90 year old Alf Groves and ending with an interview of the first female Principal Joy Gale 1975-1981. The cut off date coincided with the first Federal Government money given
to public primary schools by the Whitlam Government.
Questions related to Mordialloc Primary School and life at particular times. Each person signed a release that the interviews could be used for educational purposes to preserve the history of the school.
@graph.ldac:dateFreeText: Recording period: 1990s; Historical eras discussed: Pre-1920s, 1920s, 1930s, 1940s, 1950s, 1960s, 1970s, 1980s
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: I was a volunteer parent who initiated the project. (I have a double history major from ANU in 1970s). I attended an oral history workshop at Benga House, Heritage Hill Museum Dandenong to get some tips, bought the cassette tapes and borrowed a microphone from a friend plus a tape recorder with foot control for editing. I have original raw tapes plus edited ones used for the school's 125th Celebration where I set up listening posts in the school hall for people to listen to the recordings. I applied for funding to do more with the tapes, perhaps publish a book but no one was interested. However, I think the tapes are historically important and should be kept and made available for anyone interested and nowadays they need to be digital. I need help to digitise and also want to be sure they will be valued and looked after.
@graph.custom:otherInfo:
Oral history was a particular interest of mine and I also have some selected interviews with friends and family. However the series for Mordialloc Primary's 125th Celebration is an important historical collection with
perspectives from students, parents, cleaners, teachers ,and principals with the World Wars and Depression mentioned, polio epidemic, transition from horse to car, encounters with Pharlap (Mordialloc Primary had special dispensation to keep students to 14 years of age because those wanting to be jockeys did not intend going to high school.) At the time Mordialloc Primary was under threat of closure by the Kennett Government, I was told anything I did had to be 'cost negative' therefore I paid for all the cassettes, photographs, developing and photocopying etc.

@graph.name: Collection #48
@graph.description:
Our oral history collection is in three parts:
1. A set of approximately 30 audio cassette tapes, which are recordings of interviews and informal discussions conducted with Linton residents or former residents during the 1980s and early 1990s.
2. A set of approximately 17 CD recordings of interviews with Linton residents or former residents, conducted as part of PhD research undertaken by LDHS President Jill Wheeler, in 2005 and 2006. Jill donated these recordings to the Historical Society after she completed her research.
3. Approximately 12 audio-visual recordings on DVDs. These are recordings of talks given at LDHS 'History Nights' between 2012 and 2022. Speakers were all Linton residents or former residents, who outlined their particular family's history in Linton township and district, and recalled aspects of life in Linton as they were growing up.
@graph.ldac:dateFreeText: Recording period: 1980s, 1990s, 2000s, 2010s, 2020s; Historical eras discussed: Pre-1920s, 1920s, 1930s, 1940s, 1950s, 1960s, 1970s, 1980s, 1990s
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose:
The Linton & District Historical Society was established in 1981, and motivation for the original interviews was the realisation that many Linton 'identities' had knowledge and stories to tell that would be lost if they were not documented or recorded.
Later interviews were conducted as part of research, and talks given at LDHS 'History Nights' were filmed as a means of preserving information presented.
@graph.custom:audioFormat: Compact Cassette Tapes, Audio CDs, Digital Audio Files (WAV, mp3, etc)
@graph.custom:relatedMaterials: Other: Photographs were sometimes used to prompt memories. Where photographs are being discussed, it is often very unclear which photographs they are, and whether they are photographs from the LDHS collection, or others.
@graph.custom:outputs:
Occasionally information from the recordings is used in articles in our Society's newsletter, 'Society Notes'. e.g. information from one of the DVDs was used in the article "Another eyewitness account of adventures on the Linton school train", in Society Notes no. 316, August 2022.
The same issue of SN also had an article on a Linton 'identity', Tom Andrews (1879-1937). Some information in this article had been confirmed by listening to an interview on one of the CDs.
@graph.custom:otherInfo: Since we began to listen to and summarise the contents of these recordings (we have done this intermittently since about 2018) they are proving invaluable as a source of information about Linton's people and history, often providing new stories, and confirming information established through other research.

Yiddish culture and the history of the Kadimah
Collection protocol for Collection #61
@graph.description: Consent forms present: Yes. Consent covers future access/use: Unsure.

@graph.name: Collection #61
@graph.description:
The joint Kadimah Yiddish Book Center audio visual collection focuses on the life and Yiddish upbringing of the interviewees.  There are about 30 Melbourne interviews.  These are a fully accessible, searchable on-line resource. 
The 110th Anniversary Kadimah sound recordings relate to the interviewees' memories and experiences at the Kadimah, mostly when it was in Carlton.  There are about 20 of these.
The Kadimah archive has the full set of 3EA/SBS Yiddish programs, most of which include interviews.
The Kadimah archive has numerous sound recordings of Yiddish talks given at the Kadimah by local and overseas experts
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose:
The Yiddish Book Centre's Wexler's Oral History Project, has recorded more than 1,300 in-depth video interviews that provide a deeper understanding of the Jewish experience and the legacy and changing nature of Yiddish language and culture. About 30 of these are co-owned by the Kadimah.  In Yiddish and English, they are a fully searchable on line resource.
The Kadimah recorded (some Voice and some Zoom) oral histories as part if it 110th anniversary, wanting to capture memories and experiences before this generation died.
@graph.custom:audiovisualFormat: Digital Files (MP4, mov, etc), DVD-R (Digital Versatile Disk Recordable)
@graph.custom:outputs:
https://www.kadimah.org.au/tales-of-yiddish-melbourne-now-available-on-demand/
"Tales of Yiddish Melbourne" was made by the Kadimah and features the Yiddish Book Center's Melbourne oral histories.
@graph.custom:otherInfo:
Our oral history work is ongoing with volunteers continuing to record the lives of Yiddish speakers in Melbourne.  As we approach our 120th Anniversary we would love to be able to share them with the local and global Yiddish community and others.

As a small precariously funded community organisation we struggle to continue gathering these unique stories.

We have funding from the Publc Records Office Victoria for preserving our archive (including our oral histories) and making it accessible.

Explore the social and political history of Brunswick (Victoria, Australia) in the tumultuous years of the 1930s Depression via two audio tours by historians Melinda Barrie and Iain McIntyre.
Lock Out the Landlords : Brunswick in the Depression.
Explore the social and political history of Brunswick (Victoria, Australia) in the tumultuous years of the 1930s Depression. Join local historian Iain McIntyre to explore the collective organising of workers, families and residents in Brunswick as they fight for their survival.
Audio recorded by Nicole Hurtubise, as part of the People's Tours of History project produced by Jane Curtis with 3CR Community Radio. http://peoplestour.net/ .
Echoes tour produced by Moreland City Libraries.
Noel Counihan and the Brunswick free speech fight.
Join historian and archivist Melinda Barrie as she takes you in the footsteps of Noel Counihan and the Brunswick free speech fight. From the theatres, factories and soap-boxes of Brunswick; to dissidents riding atop street-cars and locking themselves in cages to escape arrest. Brunswick was a place of mass mobilisation and dissent.
Tour developed by Melinda Barrie, produced by Teishan Ahearne with Moreland City Libraries.

@graph.name: Michele Matthews Collection: The 1930s Depression Tapes
@graph.description:
In 2005 Michele Matthews interviewed current and former residents of Bendigo for her thesis on the 1930s Depression as part of her PhD Research, with the University of Melbourne Faculty of Arts, History Department. Michele interviewed fifteen members of prominent Bendigonian families. The thesis was titled: "How
Depressing?: The impact of the Great Depression on Bendigo and District 1925-1935".
The Michele Matthews Collection contains fourteen audio cassette tape recordings and one written transcript. The tapes are each approximately 1 hour and 45 minutes duration. The questions presented to the interviewees explore everyday experiences they would have lived through at the time of the Depression.
The questions covered sixteen relevant subjects:
• Introduction and housing
• Social activities, swimming, cinemas, school and games played with friends
• Organizations such as the RSL, Relief Committees etc. and any effect they had on family life
• The life of the single woman
• Unemployment and Employment
• Government Assistance
• Charities Aid
• Self-help
• People Power, e.g. boycotts or protests
• Politics
• Trouble or crime
• Family Health, Medical and Diet
• Education
• General memories of, and hardships encountered, during the Depression
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: In 2005 Michele Matthews interviewed current and former residents of Bendigo for her thesis on the 1930s Depression as part of her PhD Research, with the University of Melbourne Faculty of Arts, History Department.

@graph.custom:outputs: exhibitions, radio programs or podcasts):

@graph.name: Collection protocol for THE BREATH OF THE WOK: MELBOURNE'S EARLY CHINESE RESTAURANTS
@graph.description: Consent forms present: Yes. Consent covers future access/use: Unsure.

@graph.name: THE BREATH OF THE WOK: MELBOURNE'S EARLY CHINESE RESTAURANTS
@graph.description:
These oral histories are part of a thesis "THE BREATH OF THE WOK: MELBOURNE’S EARLY CHINESE RESTAURANTS" that explores Melbourne’s Chinese restaurants and their relationship with the city from the late 1900s to the early 1950s. Apart from acknowledgement and commentary in historical recipe books by Chinese
Australians, these businesses are dismissed in a line or two whenever new culinary and general histories appear. Recorded as socially isolated and frequented by prostitutes, gamblers, drunks and other unrepresentative social groups, they are recorded as ‘uncomfortable’ places for respectable non-Chinese Melburnians to patronise. Despite almost continuous growth from the late nineteenth century, they do not gain currency until the 1950s when they come to the fore as part of the post-war socio-cultural and economic success story.
@graph.ldac:dateFreeText: Recording period: 2000s; Historical eras discussed: Pre-1920s, 1920s, 1930s, 1940s, 1950s
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: This collection was created to record information from people familiar with the topic of the thesis.

@graph.description: An assorted collection of tapes and video content including interviews with members of the Chinese Australian community. The collection is broken down into a few different smaller collection categories, based on their purpose of recording.

Diana Giese’s interviews were conducted as part of the ‘Courage and Service’ project in 1997, a project which focused on the contribution of Chinese Australian servicemen and women. The project resulted in an exhibition and book, for which the oral histories formed part of the research.
Joan Jack, former GDM Curator and wife of GDM founder Russell Jack AM, conducted and supervised several interviews with Chinese Bendigodians and people associated with the Dragon Parade tradition around the time the Museum opened in 1991. The interviews date from 1980s-1990s.
The Golden Dragon Museum has undertaken further oral history recordings in more recent years, to add to the existing collection. These were not necessarily collected as part of a specific project or on any particular theme.

@graph.name: David Harris
@graph.description: Category: Individual

@graph.name: Collection protocol for Time and Place
@graph.description: Consent forms present: No. Consent covers future access/use: Not Applicable.

@graph.name: Time and Place
@graph.description: The collection is a series of cassette recordings of a 30 minute program called 'Time and Place' that went to air on a Monday night between 9.00 and 9.30 on Radio 3RRR. It ran between 1985 and 1987 having grown out of a program called 'The History Show' which moved from 3RRR to 3CR during 1985. Time and Place was less academic in style and presentation than 'The History Show' and it included interviews with authors from a range of backgrounds, reviews of books and magazines, discussions of current issues in history education, archive and heritage preservation and short interviews connected with the oral history of places and sites in Melbourne. The quality of each show varied considerably but it allegedly out-rated ABC radio on a Monday night in the 9.00-9.30 time slot. The tapes are generally in good condition and some have been digitised. Aside from significant interviews the tapes also provide an insight into an aspect of public radio in Melbourne during the 1980s.
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: The shows were taped so we could review what worked and what didn't. None of us had any training in radio presentation or production so each programme was a learning experience.
@graph.custom:audioFormat: Compact Cassette Tapes, Digital Audio Files (WAV, mp3, etc)
@graph.custom:otherInfo: My intention has been to write an article about the programme with discussion about some of the more memorable interviews and segments. I am unsure of how or if copyright is relevant as all the programmes are live broadcasts. Programmes were put together quickly each week and as this was live radio it captures a style of presentation that is lost when something is pre-recorded or presented as a podcast – or when one of the presenters is also not the show's producer. We had a deliberate focus to make the segments widely accessible and we often cited the old adage about radio being the theatre of the mind so we tried to avoid the style of broadcast that was a lecture on the radio.

A number of the interviews from the first set are also relevant to the PhD research.

@graph.name: Collection #123
@graph.description:
It is a collection of 21 interviews with mainly the women who were the subjects or witnesses in my PhD thesis, which was a history of women teachers in the Education Department of Victoria over time (1880s to 1980s). They were chosen as they added to Victoria's social capital, as they were also feminists, fought for equal pay,
pioneering principals and inspectors, first women Ministers of Education, politicians, one of whom was Joan Kirner for her leadership of parents organisations and the first women premier, unionists, exchange teachers, patriotic fund raisers, physical education leaders, curriculum writers, pioneering teachers college principal, and domestic arts teachers. Others were involved in the creation of the Australian Bicentenary's 1988 Business and Professional Women's Stained Glass Window, which I was commissioned to write about.
I also interviewed women politicians for a film, made in 1988. They have been edited and are on a video, Women in Parliament (1988), that I also directed. It was funded by the Australian Bicentenary Program.
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: As above. To provide primary sources mainly for my PhD,' "We had our own sphere", Women Teachers in the Education Department of Victoria, 1880-1980', completed in 2009. Some of the chapters were published. Other recordings were to provide primary sources for my publication, To Dream, Dare and Achieve: the history of the BPW women's window (2012). To produce the film, Women in Parliament (1988).
@graph.custom:audioFormat: Other: 'TSH' file on computer. Copied from a hand held recorder.
@graph.custom:outputs: PhD, book, book chapter, video, as discussed above.

@graph.name: History Thesis interviews

@graph.name: Wiregrass project
@graph.description: You Tube video clips featuring Percy Leason's cartoons offering a snapshot of life in the 1920s - 1960s, capturing the spirit of rural Australian utilising Kaniva's main street, social life and local characters.
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: The Wiregrass Heritage Trail aims to shine a light on the social history of rural Australia through the lens of Percy Leason's cartoons. Percy Leason, born in 1889, and in 1924 became Australia's highest-paid cartoonist. His Wiregrass series, based on the mythical town of Wiregrass, captured the essence of rural life in Australia during the twenties and thirties. This project aims to use his cartoons and story telling to engage with new audiences through a main street trail with people who may not necessarily engage with traditional art galleries and museums. NB This is a work in progress and I am still capturing stories. I hope to be finished by the end of the year, but I am trying to fit this around many other jobs and daily life.
@graph.custom:outputs: There will be a small brochure. The idea is that videos are able to be accessed via the website and also via QR codes linked to the cartoons on the trail.
@graph.custom:otherInfo:
When I started the project, I had only intended short 2 minute clips, but many people have spoken for longer, also I did not realise how many people would be keen to be part of it, so it taking me longer than anticipated as I do not have any help and it is a volunteer job.
As the administrator of the Kaniva website, many people have given me and continue to give me various historical items for the purpose of making them readily accessible. Of particular interest is this  historical recording from 1972 recorded by the ABC for a series called Heritage featuring 6 ecological regions of Australia which included the Little Desert National Park. The recording features a 45 minute interview with the 'three blind mice' and provides first hand insight into the passion of the locals in preserving the area for generations to come. The recording also features local environmentalists and local historian Les Blake, referencing the local links of poet John Shaw Nelson and the Lost in the Bush story. https://www.kaniva.org/uploads/1/3/8/2/138203694/heritage_abc_three_blind_mice_1972.m4a .
 Also of interest is an interview recorded in 1995 with Laurie Rogers as wood merchant. We have linked to his interview through our Sheep painted in his memory. https://www.kaniva.org/laurie.html . 
 Lastly, I was a founding member of the Purple Starfish and as part of our Heritage Wool Bale Stencil wall, former member Tom Curtis recorded oral histories of the sheep farmers. Unfortunately, the collection can not be located. I have personally searched for this which I remember being saved onto floppy disc at the time.

@graph.name: Oral history of the foundations of the Victorian community legal sector
@graph.description: The Federation of Community Legal Centres wanted to document the history of the Victorian community legal sector movement through the words of those who were there at the beginning. This movement for justice, which now includes 50 community legal centres across the state, began in the 1970s, when a small group of forward-thinking lawyers established the first community legal centre in Victoria, in an effort to address unmet legal need and to create an organisation through which they could advocate for just and fair laws. Last year, we invited some of the founding members of the sector/movement to speak freely about that time and their experiences in the nascent years of the community legal sector. The conversation was chaired by Jon Faine AM, who spent time working in community legal centres in the early 80s. The conversation lasted one hour and the audio was recorded.

@graph.name: Beech Forest History Series
@graph.description: The Beech Forest History Series is an oral history collection capturing rich, multi-perspective narratives of Beech Forest and the surrounding Otway region. It features Open Discussions 1 & 2 with Richard Collopy, Traditional Owner of Gadubanud Country, and Anthony Zappelli, President and Historian of the Otway Districts Historical Society and long-term Beech Forest resident. Their conversations explore the deep cultural, historical, and environmental layers of the region from both Indigenous and settler viewpoints. The collection also includes an audio recording of the Beech Forest Walking Tour, which provides an immersive storytelling experience through the landscape, including a powerful segment presented by a First Nations speaker.
@graph.ldac:dateFreeText: Recording period: 2020s; Historical eras discussed: Pre-1920s, 1930s, 1940s, 1950s
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: The Beech Forest History Series was created to preserve and share the diverse histories of Beech Forest and the Otway region, fostering a deeper understanding of place through both Indigenous and non-Indigenous voices. By documenting open conversations and local storytelling, the collection aims to honour Gadubanud Country, acknowledge the region's complex past, and promote cultural awareness, community connection, and intergenerational learning.

@graph.description: A large collection of local newspapers , many on microfilm, newspaper cuttings which form family files, rate books, sporting files, maps, some farming artefacts, an authentic shepherd's hut, War memorabilia, some clothing, approx 2500 photos which have been accessioned and none of the collection has been digitised as we have few members and all are volunteers and belong to several other voluntary organisations as well. We have several family histories and also some dvd's, tapes and a few films for which we lack equipment to show. All our members are in 60's to 80's.
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose:
To preserve the history of the town and schools and small communities around Birchip for future generations. We were able to eventually purchase and do up an old courthouse after a town centenary in 1982 when a big display of farming items and photos was held and people realised that it all needed to be collated. We try to work with students from the P12 school when we can. We do lots of research for people which brings in a little money but is very time consuming. Internet has made people research from home. Privacy rules are a bit of an issue. We have a segment from a century ago in our local paper which we began during Covid. 
We are not able to go to seminars etc in Melbourne so rely on online sessions. 
We try to have new themed displays for our town festival each year, occasional Open days and open by appointment.

@graph.name: Collection protocol for Collection #160
@graph.description: Consent forms present: No. Consent covers future access/use: Not Applicable.

@graph.name: Collection #160
@graph.description: The history of migrant settlement, focusing on several waves of arrivals, in the Springvale region.  The nationality of migrants interviewed include British, southern European, and south-east Asian (including refugees from Vietnam and Cambodia). There are more than 200 detailed interviews conducted between 1990-1993, and a second series of interviews, totalling nearly 30 conducted in 2023. All of the second series of interviews have been professionally transcribed, also close to 100 of the first series.
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose:
The first series of interviews were collected with funding from the Australian Research Council and were utilised in the publication 'Fourteen Lives'. The objective was to record the experiences of different immigrant waves, including their lived experiences in an ethnically diverse community. 

The second series was funded by the Scanlon Foundation and its objective was to record experiences in an ethnically diverse community during the COVID pandemic.
@graph.custom:audioFormat: Compact Cassette Tapes, DAT (Digital Audio Tapes), The first series of interviews (1990-93) are recorded on Compact Cassette Tapes, the second series (2023) are digitally recorded. The formats are mp4, mpeg, m4a
@graph.custom:relatedMaterials: Other: There are research materials that were collected, including some records of organisations.
@graph.custom:outputs:
Two publications. 

 Book: Andrew Markus and Eileen Sims, 'Fourteen lives: paths to a multicultural community' (1993).

Booklet, online source: Trish Prentice and Andrew Markus, 'Strong communities don't just happen. Springvale's story' (2024) Scanlon Foundation Research Institute, Narrative 12.  Available at
https://www.communityhubs.org.au/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/SFRI-Narrative-12-final-copy.pdf

@graph.description: Consent forms present: In some cases. Consent covers future access/use: No.

@graph.name: Collection of interviews of former soldiers in the Australian 2/2nd Pioneer Battalion
@graph.description:
A collection of interviews conducted by Jim Mitchell in the 1990s to facilitate his writing of a battalion history of the Australian 2/2nd Pioneer Battalion. The soldiers enlisted in 1940-41, trained in Australia and Palestine (1940-41), fought the Vichy French in Syria (1941), fought the Japanese on Java (1942), became prisoners of war and a labouring force of the Japanese in South-East Asia (where they worked on the Burma-Thailand Railway) and in Japan (1942-45). 
Recordings vary from cassette tapes to DAT; some are also made into mp3 files preparatory to being transcribed.
Transcriptions are typed versions of the interviews, but without time indications.
@graph.custom:collectionPurpose: A collection of interviews conducted by Jim Mitchell in the 1990s to facilitate his writing of a battalion history of the 2/2nd Pioneer Battalion
@graph.custom:audioFormat: Compact Cassette Tapes, DAT (Digital Audio Tapes)
@graph.custom:relatedMaterials: Photographs of interviewees, Other photographs, Written sources (e.g. diaries, letters)
@graph.custom:outputs: James Mitchell, Men at War: A social history of the Australian 2/2nd Pioneer Battalion, Vol 1: Australia, Syria, Java 1940-1942, Hardie Grant, Melbourne, 2023

The older parts of the collection are not well documented. Interviews currently being undertaken and stored in digital format are better documented, have consent forms etc.  
Ideally we would like to digitise the material held in analogue format but the time involved and/or potential cost have resulted in the project receiving a low priority.
